select-columns.label: Selecting Specific Columns
select-columns.summary: Learn how to retrieve only the columns you need.

sketch2.title: Selecting All Columns vs. Specific Columns

intro-select-columns.title: Why Select Specific Columns?
intro-select-columns.bodyMarkdown: When working with SQL tables, you often don't need every column in your results. Selecting only the columns you need makes your queries faster and your results easier to read. For example, if you only want to see the product names and prices from the `products` table, you can specify those columns in your `SELECT` statement.

syntax-select-columns.title: Syntax: Selecting Columns
syntax-select-columns.bodyMarkdown:
To select specific columns, list them after the `SELECT` keyword, separated by commas. For example:

```sql
SELECT name, price FROM products;
```

This query returns only the `name` and `price` columns from the `products` table.

all-columns-vs-specific.title: Selecting All Columns vs. Specific Columns
all-columns-vs-specific.bodyMarkdown: Using `SELECT * FROM products;` returns all columns in the table. However, it's better to specify only the columns you need, like `SELECT name, category FROM products;`. This keeps your queries efficient and your results focused.

single-choice-why-select-columns.title: Why select specific columns?
single-choice-why-select-columns.prompt: What is a main benefit of selecting only specific columns in a SQL query?

help.concept: Selecting only the columns you need reduces the amount of data processed and shown.
help.hint_1: Fewer columns means less data to handle and view.
help.hint_2: This practice improves performance and makes results simpler to understand.

options.a: It makes queries run faster and results easier to read.
options.b: It changes the data in the table.
options.c: It increases the number of rows returned.
options.d: It hides errors in your data.

multi-choice-select-columns-syntax.title: Identifying correct SELECT syntax
multi-choice-select-columns-syntax.prompt: Which of the following are correct ways to select the `name` and `price` columns from the `products` table?
multi-choice-select-columns-syntax.hint: Pay attention to commas and keywords.

help.concept: SQL requires columns to be separated by commas in the SELECT clause.
help.hint_1: Check if the columns are separated by the correct answer comma.
help.hint_2: The correct syntax is `SELECT column1, column2 FROM table;`.

options.d: SELECT name AND price FROM products;

drag-reorder-select-columns.title: Ordering a SELECT statement
drag-reorder-select-columns.prompt: Arrange the tokens to form a valid SQL statement that selects the `category` and `stock` columns from the `products` table.
drag-reorder-select-columns.hint: Start with the correct answer, then list columns, then the correct answer and the table name.

help.concept: A the correct answer statement starts with the correct answer, lists columns, then the correct answer and the table.
help.hint_1: the correct answer comes first, followed by the columns.
help.hint_2: Don't forget the the correct answer clause before the table name.

fill-blank-choice-select-columns.title: Filling in the blank: SELECT statement
fill-blank-choice-select-columns.prompt:
Fill in the blank to select only the `name` column from the `products` table:

SELECT ___ FROM products;
fill-blank-choice-select-columns.hint: Which column do you want to see in the result?

help.concept: To select a single column, put its the missing term after SELECT.
help.hint_1: The blank should be the column you want.
help.hint_2: You want only the product names, not all columns.

code-input-select-multiple-columns.title: Write a query: select multiple columns
code-input-select-multiple-columns.prompt: Write a SQL query to show the `name`, `category`, and `price` columns from the `products` table.
code-input-select-multiple-columns.hint: List the columns you want after SELECT, separated by commas.

help.concept: To select multiple columns, list them after SELECT and separate with commas.
help.hint_1: The format is SELECT column1, column2, column3 FROM table;
help.hint_2: Be sure to include all three columns in the SELECT clause.
